text: This video shows bronchoscopic examination of the airways following tumor debulking.
The right lower and middle lobes can be seen with their patent bronchi.
We begin by dividing the inferior pulmonary ligament using the hot shears before turning our attention to the oblique fissure and freeing up the right middle lobe.
Complete division of adhesions will be integral to the success of a sleeve resection as the bronchial anastomosis must be performed in a tension-free manner
Therefore, the middle and lower lobes must be completely mobilized.
After the middle and lower lobes are released, the lung is retracted anteriorly and the mediastinal pleura is incised beneath the azagus vein to expose the station 7 subcarinal lymph nodes.
The lymph node packet is gently grasped with the catio forceps, holding the capsule of the lymph nodes rather than the lymph nodes themselves directly.
An appropriate amount of force is used to appropriately manipulate the capsule without causing undue trauma.
The lymph nodes are dissected with the use of the Maryland bipolar forceps using precise motions with the bipolar energy.
In some cases, the vagal fibers to the lower lobe must be divided in order to fully skeletonize this lymph node packet.
Complete excision of the subcarinal lymph nodes is of critical importance in the sleeve lobectomy.
as this is required in order to skeletonize the bronchus intermedius.
A finger is cut from a sterile glove and inserted into the chest cavity to aid with retrieval of the specimen.
The posterior portion of the oblique fissure is divided completely to aid with exposure of the hilar structures.
After this fissure is completely divided, the interlobar lymph node packet of station 11 becomes visible.
This lymph node packet will straddle the bifurcation of the right upper lobe bronchus and the bronchus intermedius.
This lymph node station is of critical importance.
as the right upper lobe bronchus and posterior ascending artery lie deep to this interlobar lymph node packet, and its complete excision is required to fully skeletonize the bronchus intermedius.
Once these nodes are removed, the tension is turned to the hilum of the lung, where the hilar lymph nodes are dissected.
These nodes are highly inflammatory due to the long-standing bronchial obstruction caused by the tumor.

Therefore, the contour of the lymph node packet is followed closely to ensure full excision and to reveal the underlying hilar structures, such as the basilar artery and the superior segmental artery.
More anteriorly, these nodes are seen to overlie the right middle lobe artery.
The basilar trunk and the superior segmental artery are well defined after the hilar lymph nodes are fully removed.
The lung is again retracted anteriorly and attention turned to the bronchus intermedius.

In preparation for open bronchotomy, betadine-soaked rolled gauze is used, and the anesthetist passes a suction catheter into the middle and lower lobes to evacuate any secretions, which may contaminate the operative field.
The open bronchotomy is performed with hot shears at the origin of the bronchus intermedius, just distal to the takeoff of the right upper lobe bronchus.
The bronchotomy must be performed accurately in the plane perpendicular to the bronchus to avoid any shelving, as an oblique transection may lead to a compromised bronchial anastomosis.
The hot shears allow for a combination of sharp dissection and monopolar energy for hemostasis.
The remainder of the bronchus intermedius is well skeletonized from the surrounding structures.
This is only possible if thorough lymph node dissection has already been performed.
The internal surface of the bronchus intermedius is inspected, where one can see the origin of the middle and lower lobe bronchi.
Based on preoperative CT imaging,
and bronchoscopic examination the distal margin of the resection had been predetermined to be at the origin of the right middle lobe bronchus.
The bronchus intermedius is marked externally after endobronchial examination and the remainder of the bronchus is excised.
Due to the risk of recurrence it is imperative that clear operative margins are obtained
Therefore the specimen is sent for frozen section and if the margins are positive then further resection can be undertaken.
A suction catheter is again placed down into the lower lobe to remove any secretions.
A tension suture is first placed in the posterior margin of the bronchial tree.
To bring the two ends together
and reduce tension on the subsequent sutures.
Sutures are prone to tearing through the membranous portion of the airway.
Therefore, this tension suture must be placed in the cartilaginous portion of the bronchus, just adjacent to the membranous portion.
Once the two bronchial stumps are opposed, a second V-lock suture can be used to begin the anastomosis.
The bronchial anastomosis is performed with a V-lock suture to provide unidirectional tension.
The suture will start from outside to inside the bronchus so that the knot is tied externally.

Once the knot is tied down, the suture continues around the cartilaginous portion of the bronchus, as starting with the membranous portion would lead to excessive tension and the sutures tearing through.
The tension suture that was placed at the beginning can either be tied or cut.
A second V-log suture is used to commence the anastomosis through the membranous portion.
This is only initiated once sufficient tension has been taken off the membranous portion by completing an adequate number of throws with the first suture.
The V-lock suture can now be passed through the membranous part of the bronchus and used to oppose the edges without tearing through.
The anastomosis is continued and tension on the anastomosis is constantly reassessed.
When the tension is too great on one side of the anastomosis,
we switch to the other side and bring the two ends together.
The bronchial margins are gradually opposed, taking advantage of the unidirectional tension offered by the V-lock sutures.
Once the anastomosis is complete, the needles are cut and the suture is tied.
To reinforce the anastomosis, 3-O-proline is used between the previous bites and tied securely.
An inflation test is performed which shows a leak between the bites of the anastomosis.
To address this, an organic pledget of mediastinal pleura is raised and a proline suture used to secure this pleural pledget in a figure-of-eight configuration.
To secure the pleura over the anastomosis, the pleural plaget is passed through the loop of the figure of eight suture, and the needle is passed through the plaget before being tied down.
A second proline suture is used to ensure the plaget is well secured over the bronchial anastomosis.
A repeat inflation test is performed which confirms that there is no longer any leak from the bronchial anastomosis and all three lobes of the right lung are inflating.
